text: The surgeon begins the vessel-first medial-to-lateral approach for a right hemicolectomy. The ileocolic pedicle is lifted, and an incision is made below it to develop a plane underneath. The artery and vein are identified, ligated, and divided. The mesentery is lifted, and medial-to-lateral mobilisation is performed using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, reaching the bowel laterally and superiorly. The duodenum is separated from the mesentery, and lateral attachments are taken down to complete the dissection.

The video shows the supracolic approach to right hemicolon mobilization. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg with the right side elevated. The assistant holds the stomach or gastroepiploic vessels, while the surgeon incises the omentum below these vessels to enter the lesser sac. Dissection follows the gastroepiploic vessels towards the pancreas, dividing small branches to the colon as needed. A bloodless plane is found between the duodenum and transverse colon mesentery, leading to the hepatic flexure. Near the base of the transverse colon mesentery, a venous tributary joins the gastroepiploic vein from the gastrocolic trunk of Henle, which is carefully isolated and divided to expose the head of the pancreas. The hepatic flexure is taken down, and the third part of the duodenum is exposed, completing the dissection.
